definition: A codex is a book made of bound leaves — the page-turning form that replaced the scroll, and the ancestor of every modern book.
history: Latin *codex* (older *caudex*), 'tree-trunk, block of wood': before paper, the codex was a stack of wax-covered wooden tablets bound at one edge. The shift from scroll to codex in late antiquity was a media revolution — random access, two-sided pages, portability. Magic uses 'Codex' for artifacts of stored knowledge.
commentary: Why did the bound *codex* replace the scroll? It is one of book history's great puzzles. Colin Roberts and T. C. Skeat, in *The Birth of the Codex*, showed the shift was led disproportionately by early Christians, who adopted the codex for scripture far ahead of the wider book trade — but the reason stays disputed: sheer convenience, a deliberate break from Jewish scroll-culture, or the prestige of one early authoritative codex. The field treats the codex not as a natural improvement that simply won, but as a contingent choice whose cause is still argued.

commentary_sources.label: Colin H. Roberts & T. C. Skeat, The Birth of the Codex

etymology.origin: Latin codex / caudex ‘tree-trunk, block of wood’
etymology.note: Before paper, the codex was a stack of wax-covered wooden tablets bound at one edge — the direct ancestor of the page-turning book, versus the rolled scroll.
etymology.hook: The book began as a woodpile.

"Born under the sun, the first child will seek the foundation of honor and be fortified by its righteousness."
—<mark>Codex</mark> of the Constellari

"All is the hand that weaves the Fabric of Being. The Fabric of Being is All."
—*Drix <mark>Codex</mark>*, line 1
